title: Burgerwetenschap voor Pandemische Paraatheid: kansen, randvoorwaarden en stappen vooruit
author: Remmers, Gaston
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This report is the result of research on the opportunities and preconditions for citizen science for pandemic preparedness. The report is in Dutch, and includes an extended executive summary in English.   
  Citizens want to be involved in policy development, even during a pandemic or crisis. However, during the corona pandemic, there were hardly any methods and protocols available to allow citizens to actively participate in the knowledge development on which the policy was based.  
  Citizen Science encompasses a wide range of participatory research approaches, in which citizens play various roles in the development of knowledge. Thinking through the opportunities and preconditions for the use of Citizen Science during a pandemic is therefore an essential part of pandemic preparedness.  
  The aim of this project was to investigate the opportunities and preconditions for citizen science in pandemic preparedness and to gain insight into the steps that need to be taken in the 'cold phase' of a pandemic. Social and academic organizations worked together in the research. During 2024, 14 interviews (n=20) and 4 interactive workshops were held. Creative methods were used, such as a citizen science journey. We asked participants to design the ideal form of citizen science through narratives and drawings. Participants were citizens, patients, academics, professionals and policy makers.  
   Results   
  The exploration shows that there are many opportunities for citizen science to contribute to pandemic preparedness, from multiple angles. The report contains various examples of realized and potential citizen science projects for pandemic preparedness.  
  To successfully use citizen science for pandemic preparedness, five preconditions have been identified.  
  1)        strengthening the public and professional appreciation of citizen science.  
  2)        greater appreciation of the plurality of knowledge development..  
  3)        strengthening the research skills of citizens is essential.  
  4)        a data infrastructure that is usable for researchers and citizens.  
  5)        strong collaboration and decision-making structures for citizen science.  
      Conclusion and recommendations   
  Citizen science offers opportunities for pandemic preparedness. In fact, using the investigative capacities of citizens before, during and after a pandemic will probably lead to a broader repertoire of policy options, greater support for the measures, and a greater sense of control among citizens. The opportunities mentioned can only be realised in the hectic period of a pandemic if the preconditions are implemented in the cold phase of the pandemic.  
  That is why it is important to invest in a good infrastructure for citizen science, both in its social, material and technological significance, well before a pandemic, and in general, well before a disaster.  
  This leads to 3 recommendations:  
  1)        Create interconnected hubs for citizen science, spread across regions, social layers and bodies by connecting with and strengthening existing organisations  
  2)        Enable citizen participation in knowledge development by:  
  a.         Developing education and training opportunities for citizens (and professionals);  
  b.         The development of a data infrastructure that is accessible not only to professionals but also to citizens, so that citizen-generated data becomes available and usable, including narrative data;  
  c.         Understanding and utilizing the socio-psychological dynamics during a pandemic from the perspective of concepts such as Post-Traumatic Growth.  
  3)        Embrace the plurality of research methods so that a broad range of knowledge and expertise based on practice becomes available. To this end, strengthen the narrative of 'Practice Based Evidence', to legitimize and provide a framework for the pragmatic inclusion of practical findings in the field of evidence.
